title: Can Argentina's Javier Milei evolve from disruptor to political leader?
gist: Milei has delivered a genuine macro turnaround in Argentina (fiscal surplus, inflation from 211% to ~30%, rebuilt FX reserves) but the gains are concentrated in a few export sectors, political fatigue and coalition infighting are rising, and a resurgent Peronism under Cristina Fernández de Kirchner and Axel Kicillof is again a viable alternative.

insights.claim: Milei enacted one of the most ambitious fiscal adjustments in modern history, eliminating Argentina's fiscal deficit and restoring a budget surplus.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A rare, credible sovereign fiscal consolidation case worth benchmarking against other EM fiscal-adjustment attempts.

insights.claim: Inflation in Argentina fell from 211% when Milei took office in December 2023 to just over 30% as of the article's writing (mid-2026), and no longer dominates political debate even though it remains among the highest rates globally.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A large, verifiable disinflation that materially changes the domestic political conversation, even if inflation is still elevated by global standards.
insights.locator: Inflation has come down from 211 per cent

insights.claim: Argentina's economy is expected to grow 3.5% this year, boosted by shale oil and gas exports from the Vaca Muerta reserves, plus mining and agriculture.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Growth is real but narrowly sourced from a handful of export sectors, a concentration risk for the recovery's durability.
insights.locator: The economy is expected to grow by 3.5 per cent

insights.claim: Argentina's central bank has successfully defended the peso's value and begun rebuilding foreign currency reserves, addressing one of the country's most persistent economic vulnerabilities.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: FX reserve rebuilding is a leading indicator of reduced balance-of-payments crisis risk for Argentina.
insights.locator: Argentina's central bank has successfully defended

insights.claim: Despite his close political affinity with Donald Trump, Milei has pursued trade liberalization rather than protectionism, slashing export taxes and supporting the Mercosur-EU free trade agreement.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Milei's trade policy diverges from the protectionist drift of his closest political ally, signalling ideological consistency over alliance mimicry.
insights.locator: Finally, despite his close political affinity

insights.claim: After Milei's party lost the September 2025 Buenos Aires provincial elections, market turbulence raised serious doubts about the sustainability of his programme, prompting a direct intervention from the US Treasury to restore confidence.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Argentina's reform programme is still fragile enough that a single provincial election result can trigger a market crisis requiring external backstop.
insights.locator: But in September 2025, following his party's defeat

insights.claim: Argentina is still struggling to lower its sovereign risk premium and re-enter international debt markets, one of the government's most important unresolved medium-term objectives.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Sovereign risk premium and market re-access are the key metrics to watch for whether Milei's stabilization becomes durable investment-grade credibility.
insights.locator: While those fears have subsided somewhat

insights.claim: Growth has concentrated in a handful of highly competitive export sectors (agriculture, mining, oil and gas) while less competitive sectors like manufacturing struggle, and much of the new employment created is informal or low-paying, such as delivery services.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Macro stabilization is not translating into broad-based wage or employment gains, which is the likely source of Milei's approval decline.
insights.locator: Real wages remain low, consumption has recovered unevenly

insights.claim: The government is betting that lower inflation, deregulation and fiscal discipline will eventually unlock a broader investment wave, but it is uncertain whether Argentinians are willing to wait for those promised benefits to materialize.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The pace of any investment-led broadening of gains is the key variable determining whether political patience holds through the next elections.
insights.locator: The government is betting that lower inflation

insights.claim: Milei is highly effective at setting economic direction but has shown less interest in the day-to-day management of government outside economic policy, a structural limitation now being exposed as his honeymoon period ends.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Non-economic governance failures, not economic policy itself, are the most likely proximate driver of any political setback for Milei.

insights.claim: Milei has been unable to resolve a widening public dispute between his sister and closest confidant Karina Milei and chief political strategist Santiago Caputo, fuelling perceptions of infighting, and he has stood by chief of staff Manuel Adorni despite corruption allegations, including Adorni's own admission of underreporting his taxes.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Internal loyalty conflicts and corruption optics are undermining Milei's core anti-establishment brand, a political rather than economic risk to monitor.
insights.locator: Milei is fiercely loyal to his small circle

insights.claim: Cristina Fernández de Kirchner, despite a corruption conviction and house arrest that ended her ability to run for office, is once again polling competitively, showing Peronism retains real electoral pull.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A disqualified former leader polling competitively signals Peronism could still shape the next electoral cycle even without her on the ballot.
insights.locator: Former president Cristina Fernández de Kirchner

insights.claim: Buenos Aires governor Axel Kicillof, though his relationship with Kirchner is strained, is the leading contender to inherit her mantle; he is closely associated with the interventionist model Milei was elected to dismantle and would be a formidable candidate only if Argentinians decide Milei's experiment needs reversing rather than moderating.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Kicillof's electoral viability is a direct read on whether Argentine voters want to reverse or merely soften Milei's reforms.
insights.locator: The leading contender to inherit her mantle
